explanation: Most accessory canals are in the apical third. Common board figures cite ~17% teeth with apical accessory canals (texts also emphasize apical location prevalence).
Narrowest canal diameter is usually the apical constriction (minor foramen / near CDJ region).
TST induration cutoffs depend on risk (≥5/10/15 mm). Low-risk often ≥15 mm; higher-risk lower thresholds.
Recent transplant: immunocompromised — coordinate care; often defer elective care, manage infection risk, treat disease thoughtfully.
Odontogenic infections are typically mixed aerobic/anaerobic oral flora.
Normal SNA with decreased SNB → mandibular retrognathia (skeletal Class II pattern).
Food impaction after crown often from open/light proximal contact or poor contour/emergence.
Classic biologic width / supracrestal attachment ≈ 2 mm (JE + CT). ~3 mm if sulcus included in some teachings.
Informed consent: diagnosis, proposed treatment, risks/benefits, alternatives, and opportunity for questions.
Ludwig angina: bilateral submandibular/sublingual/submental floor-of-mouth infection — airway emergency.
